key_insight: PriorProof measures how nonstandard a formal proof route is at a specific time by scoring the surprisal of its dependency footprint against an earlier snapshot of Mathlib.
engineering_relevance: It gives theorem-proving systems a decomposable, label-free novelty signal that can help rank proofs, compare techniques, and flag when a proof path is unusually unfamiliar.
method: Extract the proof term’s dependency footprint from Lean.
Learn statement retrieval from proof-derived contrastive pairs.
Score footprint surprisal with a retrieval-conditioned, hierarchically smoothed prior built from an earlier Mathlib snapshot.
evidence_signal: In a blinded topology study, PriorProof matched the majority of domain raters on 53/76 paired judgments (69.7%), with 11/12 canonical contrasts correct and a non-significant paired difference versus the best language-model condition (McNemar p = 0.210).
watch_item: The signal is time-relative and depends on the quality of retrieval and the chosen historical snapshot, so performance may shift as Mathlib evolves or if proof-term parsing is brittle.
paper_title: PriorProof: A Point-in-Time Measure of Technique Novelty for Formal Proofs
